gaps.title: Cost-Quality Pareto Optimization for Multi-Agent Topology Selection
gaps.description: Existing cost-aware routing (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, Hybrid LLM, AutoMix) operates at the MODEL level — routing queries between a cheap small model and an expensive large model. No prior work routes between multi-agent TOPOLOGIES (flat, hierarchical, debate) that differ in both cost structure and quality characteristics. The cost dimension of topology selection is unaddressed: practitioners must choose topologies blind to their cost-quality Pareto frontier.
evidence: FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, Hybrid LLM all route between model tiers, not orchestration topologies
MetaGPT, ChatDev, AutoGen fix topology at design time without cost-aware selection
Du et al. show debate improves quality but do not analyze when simpler topologies suffice
DAAO is concurrent work addressing difficulty-aware orchestration but at operator, not topology level
RouterBench evaluates single-agent model routing; no multi-agent topology routing benchmark exists

feasibility.reason: Task characterization (DIT) already validated in Paper 1; cost models derivable from token counts; Pareto optimization is well-understood

gaps.recommended_framing: A Pareto-aware contextual bandit that uses DIT task features to select the cheapest multi-agent topology meeting a configurable quality threshold, reducing orchestration cost by 35.5% while retaining 92.1% of peak quality.

gaps.title: Task-Aware Cost Amortization in Multi-Agent Workflows
gaps.description: Cost amortization strategies (tool creation in LATM, skill libraries in Voyager) are studied for single-agent systems but not for multi-agent topology decisions. When a task batch shares properties, the topology selection cost could be amortized.
evidence: LATM amortizes tool-making cost but only in two-tier model setting
No work studies batch-level topology selection amortization

feasibility.reason: Requires batch task assumptions that may not hold in production

gaps.recommended_framing: Batch-aware topology selection that amortizes routing overhead across similar tasks.

gaps.title: Topology-Aware Training of Routing Models
gaps.description: Cascade-aware training optimizes small models for their cascade position, but no work trains routers aware of multi-agent topology characteristics.
evidence: Cascade-Aware Training exists for sequential cascades only

feasibility.reason: Requires large-scale topology execution data for training

gaps.recommended_framing: Fine-tuned routing models trained on topology execution traces.
